title: Wiesberger Ends the Drought: Volvo China Open Victory in Shanghai
excerpt: Bernd Wiesberger claimed his first DP World Tour title in nearly five years with a commanding performance at Shanghai's Volvo China Open, finishing 19-under par.
body:
Five Years of Waiting, One Perfect Sunday

Bernd Wiesberger ended a five-year drought with a stirring final round performance to claim his ninth career DP World Tour title at the Volvo China Open in Shanghai on Sunday. The Austrian shot a composed 67 in the final round to finish 19-under par, a three-shot victory over overnight leader Adrian Otaegui. It was more than just a win — it was redemption for a player who had drifted in and out of the spotlight since his last DP World Tour victory.

Winning Score
-19
Margin of Victory
3 shots
Final Round
67
Prize Money
€400k

The Back Nine That Changed Everything

Wiesberger entered the final round one shot behind Otaegui at Shanghai Enhance Anting Golf Club. From the start, he was in control. His round of 67 was punctuated by steady ball-striking and cool putting when it mattered — a stark contrast to Otaegui's collapse. The Spaniard, who held the overnight lead, unravelled over the back nine with two bogeys and a double bogey, shooting 71 to surrender the title. Wiesberger's decisive final round cemented what had been building all week: a complete return to form.

Five Years Is a Long Time

For context, Wiesberger's last DP World Tour victory came in 2021. In between, he has watched the landscape of professional golf shift dramatically — the rise of LIV Golf, the reshuffling of the tours, the migration of star players. He was not among the defectors, and on Sunday at Steyn City he proved loyalty can still be rewarded. The 36-year-old's victory is his ninth career DP World Tour title, and perhaps the sweetest given the long wait and the pedigree of his field.

The Numbers

Beyond the headline, the statistical dominance was clear. Wiesberger earned 585 Race to Dubai points and 14.8 Official World Golf Ranking points — real currency in the race to major championships. At a tournament that sits a quarter-way through the DP World Tour season, his Volvo China Open triumph stands as one of the autumn's defining moments. The prize purse was €2.75 million, with Wiesberger pocketing €400,172.05 for his efforts.

What's Next

The DP World Tour continues immediately with the Turkish Airlines Open next week. For Wiesberger, however, this victory offers something far more valuable than money or points — it offers momentum. After years of quiet competence on the margins, he has announced his return to golf's elite stage. The drought is broken. The question now is not whether he can win again, but how many more titles he will claim in the coming months.
